responses.response: The autonomy objection is noted but does not overturn our conditional stance: we never proposed dissolving maintainer distinctiveness, only binding all maintainers receiving public recognition/certification to a common non-discrimination floor in admissions criteria — a narrower ask than uniform curriculum or mission control. Subsidiarity protects institutional identity, not the capacity to become an unregulated escape valve that defeats a state-sector equity reform by design. If church/foundation maintainers can show that mission-differentiated admissions do not function as SES-sorting channels in practice (data, not assertion), the binding-mandate condition could be relaxed to a monitoring-only regime. Absent that evidence, the legal fight over autonomy is a cost we must still weigh, which is precisely why our condition_to_change already requires legal certainty before commitment — this argument sharpens that requirement, it does not remove it.

This is a real distributional concern but it cuts against S2's design, not our conditional position on it — we already flagged non-state absorption as the central unresolved risk in our S2 basis. The maintainer-viability argument and the absorption-risk argument (A7/A10) are two sides of the same coin: either non-state maintainers absorb displaced demand (undermining the equity rationale) or they cannot and face fiscal distress (undermining institutional viability). Both cannot be true at scale simultaneously, and our position already requires a capping/registry mechanism precisely to resolve this ambiguity empirically before further commitment. We would not extend per-pupil funding guarantees to non-state maintainers as compensation for demographic contraction without that data, since doing so before knowing which risk materializes would pre-commit fiscal resources against an unquantified need.

We recognize the autonomy-preserving appeal of S4, but the argument overstates its equity sufficiency by treating institutional non-disruption as equivalent to policy success. Our own conditional stance already grants that S4 is the lowest-friction option — that is exactly why we flagged its central danger: it may function to entrench rather than address sorting, precisely because it avoids the harder cross-maintainer and structural questions this argument treats as strengths. Respecting subsidiarity does not require accepting that compensation is durable or sufficient; those are separate, empirical claims this argument does not settle. We would treat S4 as legitimate within its bounded equity ambition only if paired with the sunset/monitoring conditions we and others (A18) have specified — not as a standalone permanent settlement justified by autonomy alone.

This argument reinforces rather than challenges our position — we already state explicitly that S1's equity gain is bounded and must not be framed as a system-wide fix. Where we'd push back is on the implied conclusion that boundedness makes S1 not worth pursuing: a governing majority's interest in measurable, durable progress means a bounded but real gain among applicants is still preferable to no reform, provided it is honestly scoped and not used to close the political window on addressing pre-application self-selection (rural Roma non-application, information gaps). We would add a companion outreach/awareness commitment addressing pre-application barriers as a condition alongside our existing four, so S1 is legislated as one tier of a multi-stage equity strategy rather than presented as sufficient on its own.
responses.new_condition: We would move to support S1 only if, in addition to our existing four conditions, the legislative package explicitly commits to a parallel pre-application outreach programme (targeting rural Roma and lowest-SES non-applicants) with its own monitored targets, so the reform is not presented or politically used as a complete equity fix.
